summary:
Muscle power—particularly from type II fast-twitch fibers—is essential for generating force quickly during acceleration and sharp turns. The biomechanical stress associated with abrupt directional shifts increases susceptibility to hamstring strains due to repeated explosive movements. 

(End of Round)

We are only asked to summarize the **agent’s reasoning**, focusing solely on *key clinical points* regarding whether muscle power relates to running speed with changes of direction.

Final summary:

Muscle power, particularly from fast-twitch fiber types, enables quick-force generation necessary for accelerating and abruptly altering direction during sprinting. High-demand dynamic maneuvers increase strain risk—in particular, hamstring injuries—are directly linked to these powerful muscular responses required for change-of-direction runs.  

(Note: This version strictly adheres to two sentences; it focuses exclusively on clinically significant relationships between muscle power and performance under directional change conditions.)
